1: Psalm 37 is an extended alphabetic acrostic wisdom poem: each two-verse (or occasionally three-verse) strophe opens with a successive letter of the 22-letter Hebrew alphabet, running from aleph to taw. This carefully crafted structure signals comprehensive treatment of its subject — the problem of the wicked prospering while the righteous suffer, the central theodicy question of the OT wisdom tradition. The poem belongs to the genre of wisdom psalms (also Pss 1, 19, 49, 73, 78, 112, 119, 128, 133) in which the psalmist teaches from experience rather than addressing God in petition or praise. "Of David" (superscription) — the Davidic attribution gives the speaker authority as one who has navigated the very experiences the poem addresses.
2: "For like grass they will soon be cut down; like green plants they will wither away" — the grass-and-plant metaphor for human transience is foundational to the Psalter's theodicy (Pss 90:5-6; 103:15-16; Isa 40:6-8). The metaphor does double work: the grass appears vigorous in spring but is cut before summer ends, so the wicked's flourishing prosperity is itself the evidence of its coming end. The short timescale ("soon," mĕhērāh) is the answer to the "how long?" question that underlies the whole poem. Second Temple readers would have read the wither-and-cut imagery through Isa 40's "the grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God stands forever."
3: "Trust in the LORD and do good; live in the land (šĕkon-ʾereṣ) and enjoy secure provision (rĕʿēh ʾĕmûnāh)" — the land promise is inseparable from covenant relationship throughout the Psalter and Deuteronomy. Šākan (dwell, tent, tabernacle) is the root from which šĕkînat (divine presence/dwelling) derives; to dwell in the land is to participate in YHWH's dwelling with Israel. Rĕʿēh ʾĕmûnāh is rendered "enjoy secure provision" in the MKT — literally "shepherd faithfulness"; in the pastoral economy, faithful shepherding meant reliable sustenance. The command structure is noteworthy: trust + do good = basis; land-dwelling = result. Deuteronomy's land theology is condensed here.
4: "Find your delight (hiṯʿannag) in the LORD, and he will give you the desires of your heart" — ʿānag (to be delicate, to be soft, to take sensuous delight) is an unusual word typically applied to physical luxury (Isa 55:2; 58:14). The promise that YHWH will give "the desires (mišʾălôt) of your heart" has been misread as a blank-check prosperity promise; in context it is the covenant claim that the one whose delight is in YHWH will find that YHWH himself becomes the desire of the heart — the petition and the answer converge. Augustine's our heart is restless until it rests in thee is the theological tradition that reads this correctly.
5: "Commit (gôl) your way to the LORD" — gālaṭ (roll, commit by rolling onto) gives the physical metaphor: roll your burden onto YHWH as one rolls a heavy stone from a doorway. The same root appears in Prov 16:3 ("commit your works to the LORD") and in the Septuagint's rendering of Ps 22:8 ("he committed himself to God"; cited by Matt 27:43 in the mockery of Jesus). "He will bring it to pass (yeʿăśeh)" — the confident assertion of divine efficacy for the one who trusts; not a guarantee of immediate outcome but of ultimate accomplishment of what God wills for the righteous.
6: "He will bring your righteousness to light (kāʾôr) and your cause bright as noon (kattāhorayim)" — the juridical imagery of divine vindication rendered in light metaphors. In the ancient Israelite legal system, the ultimate court of appeal for the falsely accused was YHWH himself; the divine judge would vindicate the innocent when human courts failed. "Bright as noon" — the metaphor of the noonday sun is the most intense available in a Mediterranean context; what is hidden will be made inescapably visible. Isaiah 58:8 uses the same dawn/noon sequence for the vindication of the fasting community.
7: "Be still (dôm) before the LORD and wait patiently for him (hityaḥēl)" — two imperatives demanding the opposite of what distress naturally produces. Dāmam (to be still, to be silent, to stop) is also used of the sun standing still (Josh 10:12-13) and of the earth being silent before God (Hab 2:20). Yāḥal (to wait, to hope with expectant patience) is the characteristic verb of the Psalter's theodicy response: the righteous wait for YHWH in the face of what seems like inexplicable delay (Pss 25:3, 5, 21; 27:14; 31:24; 33:20; 38:15; 39:7). The command "do not resent the one who prospers" targets the inner attitude (resentment, envy, fretting) as the primary spiritual danger of theodicy situations.
8: "Let go of anger (ʿereṣ) and abandon wrath (ḥēmāh); do not fret (titḥār)—it leads only to evil" — three parallel commands addressing the inner life during the experience of seeming injustice. Ḥēmāh (rage, burning wrath, also poison/venom) connects the internal experience of anger with the lethal potential of snake venom — both burn and destroy from within. Ḥārar (fret, chafe, be hot) is the same root as the fever-heat of burning; fretting at injustice overheats the soul. The paradox of the verse: the natural response to the wicked prospering is hot anger, but this anger is itself the spiritual danger ("leads only to evil") — it replicates the wicked's destructive spirit in the righteous.
9: "For the wicked will be cut off (yikkārētû), but those who wait for the LORD (qôwê YHWH) will inherit the earth (yîrĕšû-ʾāreṣ)" — the covenant land-inheritance promise deployed as the theodicy resolution. Qāwāh (to wait with hope, to collect strength like a cord drawn taut) is the virtue of patient expectation; Isaiah uses it for those who "renew their strength" (Isa 40:31). The land-inheritance theology runs from the patriarchal covenant (Gen 15:7; 17:8) through Deuteronomy's obedience-blessing-land structure (Deut 4:1; 6:18; 16:20) to the Psalter's eschatological use: the "earth" (or "land," ʾāreṣ) inherited by the righteous is simultaneously the promised land and the creation itself.
10: "In just a little while (ʿôd meʿaṭ) the wicked will be gone; you will search for his place, but it will not be found" — the "little while" is a wisdom assertion about historical time, not a precise prediction. Hab 2:3 uses similar temporal language: "the revelation awaits an appointed time; if it seems slow, wait for it." The searching-and-not-finding motif is the complete erasure of trace — not merely gone but untraceable. In ANE thought, the ultimate defeat was to have no place, no land, no memorial, no offspring to remember you (the root of the "cut off" language). The verse closes the theodicy circle: what the wicked sought to take from the righteous (place, inheritance, future) is precisely what the wicked loses.
11: "But the meek (ʿănāwîm) will inherit the earth and delight in abundant peace (šālôm)" — the central verse of the entire psalm, made famous by Jesus's quotation in the third Beatitude (Matt 5:5). The ʿănāwîm (meek, humble, poor in spirit) are those who have chosen not to take, grab, or scheme — the antithesis of the wicked who "scheme" (v. 12), "draw the sword" (v. 14), and "borrow without repaying" (v. 21). In Second Temple Judaism, the ʿănāwîm became a technical term for the pious remnant, the community that trusted YHWH rather than political alliances or violence (cf. Zeph 2:3; 3:12; Isa 61:1). The Qumran community likely saw themselves as the ʿănāwîm of this psalm, awaiting their eschatological inheritance.
12: "The wicked man schemes (zômēm) against the righteous and grinds his teeth (ḥāraq šinnayim) at him" — the paired acts of the wicked: inner scheming and outward physical contempt. Zāmam (to plan, scheme, devise) in the legal context means to prepare false testimony — a scheming witness (Deut 19:19). "Grinding the teeth" is the gesture of aggressive rage that the Psalter records as the enemy's response to the righteous person's trust and blessing (Pss 35:16; 112:10; Lam 2:16; Acts 7:54 — the Sanhedrin grinds teeth at Stephen's speech). The bodily expression of hatred makes the wicked's inner scheming visible in a gesture that could be observed by the community.
13: "The Lord laughs at the wicked, for he sees that their day is coming" — divine laughter at the wicked is one of the Psalter's most striking theological assertions (Ps 2:4 — YHWH laughs at conspiring kings; Ps 59:8). The laughter is not casual amusement but the triumphant confidence of the one who holds final authority: the wicked's apparent power is from the divine perspective already a settled matter. "His day is coming (yômô bāʾ)" — the day of reckoning and judgment. "Day" (yôm) language for divine judgment appears throughout the prophets (Isa 13:6; Jer 46:10; Ezek 30:3; Joel 2:1; Amos 5:18) as the "Day of the LORD" — the day when YHWH's rule becomes inescapably visible.
14: "The wicked draw the sword and bend their bow to bring down the poor (ʿānî) and helpless (ʾebyôn)" — the two most common OT designations for the socially vulnerable are paired: ʿānî (the poor, the afflicted, those without social resources) and ʾebyôn (the needy, those in genuine material want). The combination identifies the targets of the wicked's armed aggression as those with no military or social means to resist. In the ancient Israelite society, armed violence against the poor was the greatest moral offense against the covenant order — the prophets (Amos 2:6-7; 8:4-6; Isa 10:1-2; Mic 2:1-2) make this the primary charge against Israel's ruling class.
15: "Their swords will pierce their own hearts, and their bows will be shattered" — the boomerang principle of retributive justice, in which the instrument of harm turns against its wielder (Pss 7:15-16; 9:15-16; 35:8; Prov 26:27; Eccl 10:8). The sword-that-pierces-the-heart is the most intimate form of this reversal: the weapon carried against another ends up in the carrier's own chest. This retributive pattern — the wicked destroyed by their own schemes — is one of the Psalter's fundamental theological convictions about the moral order of the universe, rooted in the character of the God who oversees it.
16: "Better is the little that the righteous has than the great wealth of many wicked" — the "better than" (ṭôb … min) form is characteristic of the wisdom tradition's comparative aphorisms (Prov 15:16-17; 16:8, 19; 17:1; 19:1, 22; 21:9; 25:7, 24; Eccl 4:6; 9:16). The comparison challenges the ANE and universal assumption that wealth = blessing. In the covenant theology of the Psalter, what the righteous has — relationship with YHWH, integrity, covenant faithfulness — exceeds in value any quantity of material wealth held by those outside the covenant. This verse is the wisdom-psalm equivalent of Jesus's "what does it profit a man to gain the whole world and forfeit his soul?" (Mark 8:36).
17: "For the strength of the wicked will be shattered, but the LORD supports (sômēk) the righteous" — sāmak (to support, to lean on, to uphold) is the verb used for laying hands on the head of the sacrificial animal (Lev 1:4; 3:2; 4:4) and for taking an oath by laying hands on a person (Lev 24:14). The divine supporting hand on the righteous is thus both a sustaining grip and a covenantal identification. The antithesis with the wicked's "strength (zĕrôʿôt) being shattered" — the arm/shoulder as the symbol of military power being broken — echoes Ps 10:15; 37:37; Isa 40:10.
18: "The LORD knows (yôdēaʿ) the days of the upright, and their inheritance will last forever" — divine knowledge of "the days" is not merely temporal awareness but intimate engagement: YHWH knows the days of the righteous as a shepherd knows his flock or a parent knows a child. "Their inheritance will last forever (lĕʿôlām tihyeh naḥălāṯām)" — the covenant land inheritance is given eschatological scope: it is not merely a historical possession subject to exile and loss but an eternal endowment. This is the Psalter's answer to the Assyrian and Babylonian destructions: the exile cannot be the final word because the righteous's inheritance is eternal.
19: "They will not be disgraced in days of trouble (bĕʿēt rāʿāh); in times of famine (bîmê rāʿāb) they will be satisfied" — two specific crisis scenarios: unspecified trouble (war, social upheaval, persecution) and famine. Famine was the recurrent catastrophe of ancient Near Eastern life, striking every few years in the grain-dependent agrarian economies of Palestine and Mesopotamia. The promise of satisfaction "in famine" is the covenant provision of Deut 28:12 and its anti-type in the curse list (Deut 28:22-24, 38-42). In Joseph's narrative, YHWH uses Joseph to provide grain in Egypt during the very famine that would have destroyed the covenant family — the divine care in famine is a historical type the Psalter assumes.
20: "The wicked will perish; the enemies of the LORD will fade like green pastures (kîqar kārîm kālû)" — a difficult phrase; some manuscripts and the Septuagint read differently. The MKT follows "green pastures" — the lush spring grass that disappears in summer heat. "They will dissolve, consumed like smoke" — the smoke metaphor for complete and rapid dissolution appears in Ps 68:2 ("as smoke is driven away"); Isa 51:6 ("the heavens will vanish like smoke"). The three images — fading grass, dissolving smoke, perishing enemies — reinforce the central theodicy claim: the wicked's apparent permanence is illusory; their dissolution is as natural and complete as summer's end.
21: "The wicked borrows and does not repay, but the righteous is generous and gives" — the economic behavior of the wicked versus the righteous as moral indicators. In ancient Israelite society, lending was a covenant obligation: the Torah prohibited lending at interest to fellow Israelites (Exod 22:25; Lev 25:36-37; Deut 23:19-20) and required loans to the needy as acts of covenant solidarity. The wicked who borrows and does not repay violates both the economic trust (a contract broken) and the covenantal ethic (taking from the poor without reciprocity). The righteous who is "generous and gives" (ḥônēn ûmalbēš) enacts the covenant character of YHWH himself (cf. Ps 112:5; Prov 19:17; 28:27).
22: "Those blessed by the LORD will inherit the earth, but those he has cursed will be cut off" — the deuteronomic blessing-curse structure stated in its starkest form. The Abrahamic covenant is the paradigm: "I will bless those who bless you, and those who dishonor you I will curse" (Gen 12:3). In the Psalter, the categories of "blessed" and "cursed" are not arbitrary divine preferences but the natural outcomes of alignment with or opposition to the covenant order. "Cut off (yikkārētû)" — the covenant legal penalty for violations of the most serious covenant obligations (Exod 12:15, 19; 30:33, 38; 31:14; Lev 7:20-27), meaning exclusion from the covenant community and its future.
23: "The LORD establishes (mēkônān) the steps of a man and delights (yehpāṣ) in his way" — kûn (to establish, to make firm, to prepare) is used for YHWH's establishment of creation (Pss 96:10; 119:90), of the throne (1 Sam 13:13; 2 Sam 7:13), and of the human steps within the covenant path. The pairing with divine delight (ḥāpēṣ) makes the relationship intimate rather than merely administrative: YHWH takes pleasure in the covenant-aligned human's journey. This is the divine-shepherd-of-the-individual motif in its wisdom form, parallel to Ps 23's "he guides me in the paths of righteousness" and Prov 3:6 "he will make straight your paths."
24: "Though he stumbles (yippōl), he will not fall headlong (yûṭāl), for the LORD holds (sômēk) his hand" — the two-stage description of the righteous person's fall differentiates stumbling (the inevitable failures of the good) from headlong collapse (the final ruin of the wicked). The divine hand holding (sāmak) — the same supporting verb as v. 17 — is what converts the stumble into recovery rather than fall. In Second Temple Judaism, Prov 24:16 reinforced this teaching: "for the righteous falls seven times and rises again, but the wicked stumble into calamity." The divine-held-hand motif is one of the Psalter's most intimate anthropomorphisms.
25: "I was young and now I am old, yet I have never seen the righteous abandoned (neʾĕzāb) or their children begging for food" — the appeal to personal biography as wisdom evidence. ANE wisdom literature regularly cited the sage's lifetime observation as the basis for instruction (Prov 4:3-4; 7:6-7; 24:32; Eccl 1:12-14; Sir 51:13-22). The claim is not empirical-universal but covenantal: the psalmist is not asserting that no righteous person has ever suffered want (Elijah fled into the desert hungry; Paul goes hungry in 2 Cor 11:27) but that YHWH's pattern of provision for the covenant-faithful is consistent enough to be called never-having-seen. The community memory of divine faithfulness is the basis of present trust.
26: "He is always generous and lending (ûmalweh), and his children become a blessing (lĕbĕrākāh)" — the righteous person's generosity cascades across generations: his children become a blessing to others, continuing the chain of covenant kindness. "His children become a blessing" echoes the Abrahamic covenant formula: "you will be a blessing" (Gen 12:2) and "in your offspring all nations will be blessed" (Gen 22:18). The righteous person's life functions, in miniature, as the fulfillment of the Abrahamic mission: to be a source of blessing to the surrounding world.
27: "Turn away from evil (sûr mērāʿ) and do good, so you will live in the land permanently" — the ethical summary of the psalm's entire teaching compressed into an imperative couplet. Sûr mērāʿ (turn away from evil) appears in Ps 34:14 in almost identical form, and in Job 1:1, 8; 2:3 as the definition of Job's righteousness ("blameless and upright, one who feared God and turned away from evil"). "Live in the land permanently (lĕʿôlam šĕkon)" — the land-dwelling permanence of the covenant obedient is the Psalter's answer to the historical fact of exile. The covenant promise is not revoked by historical catastrophe but awaits its eschatological fulfillment.
28: "For the LORD loves justice (mišpāṭ); he does not abandon (lōʾ-yaʿăzōb) his faithful people (ḥăsîdāyw)" — the three divine attributes that constitute the psalm's theodicy resolution: justice-love, non-abandonment, and faithful-ones-protection. Ḥāsîd (faithful one, pious one, loyal one) — the corresponding human term to divine ḥesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty); the ḥăsîdîm are those who reciprocate divine covenant loyalty with their own loyalty. "The offspring of the wicked will be cut off (wĕzereʿ rĕšāʿîm nikkĕrāṯ)" — the generational contrast: the righteous's children are "a blessing" (v. 26) while the wicked's offspring are "cut off," ending the family line.
29: "The righteous will inherit the earth (ʾereṣ) and make their home in it forever" — the third occurrence of the land-inheritance promise in the psalm (vv. 9, 11, 29), creating an emphatic structural refrain. The eschatological scope intensifies at each repetition: "inherit the earth" and "make their home in it forever (lĕʿôlam)" push the promise beyond the historical promised land into the apocalyptic new creation. Isaiah 65:17-25 and 66:22 use similar language for the eschatological new earth. The NT citation (Matt 5:5) reads this precisely in its eschatological register — the meek do not merely settle in Canaan but receive the renewed earth of the age to come.
30: "The righteous speak wisdom (yehgeh ḥokmāh), and their tongue talks of justice (mišpāṭ)" — the inner character of the righteous is expressed in the quality of their speech. Hāgāh (to mutter, to meditate, to murmur) is used for the constant inner speech of one who meditates on Torah (Ps 1:2; Josh 1:8). The righteous person's mouth is filled with wisdom and justice because those are the categories that dominate his inner life. Wisdom literature's speech ethics: James 3's "the tongue is a fire" is the NT continuation of this tradition — the quality of speech as the measure of the inner person (Prov 10:11, 13-14, 18-21; 12:17-19; Matt 12:34).
31: "The law (tôrat) of his God is in his heart (lēb); his steps do not falter" — the internalization of Torah is the Psalter's ideal of righteous formation. Deut 6:6 ("these words shall be in your heart") and Jer 31:33 ("I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts") both describe the same internalization. In Ps 119, the 176-verse meditation on Torah, the same claim is central (Ps 119:11: "I have hidden your word in my heart that I might not sin against you"). The practical result of internalized Torah is stability: "his steps do not falter (lōʾ timlāḍ ʾăšurāyw)," reversing the "slippery path" prepared for the wicked (Ps 35:6).
32: "The wicked lie in wait for the righteous, looking to kill them (lĕhamîtô)" — the ambush metaphor for legal and social conspiracy returns (cf. Ps 10:8-9 — the wicked ambush like a lion; 35:7 — the net and pit of the conspirator). The legal context is prominent: the accusers who "lie in wait" are preparing false testimony; the "killing" may be social and legal — destroying the righteous person's reputation, property, and legal standing — rather than literal assassination. In the ancient world, legal destruction was as final as physical death: it ended a person's ability to function in the community.
33: "The LORD will not leave the righteous at the mercy (lĕyādô) of the wicked or let them be condemned at trial (bĕhiššāpĕṭô)" — the legal and divine dimensions converge: YHWH will not leave the righteous "in the hand" (legal power) of the wicked, and will not condemn them "when he [the righteous] is tried." Both clauses address the same danger from two sides. The divine guarantee of courtroom vindication is the prayer of every accused innocent in the Psalter (Pss 7:8; 26:1; 35:23-24; 43:1; 109:31). YHWH as the ultimate court is the theodicy resolution of the entire Psalm 37: the verdicts of human courts are not final; the divine tribunal overrules them.
34: "Wait for the LORD and keep his way (šĕmōr darkô); he will exalt you to inherit the earth. You will be there to see the wicked cut off" — the personal witness to divine vindication: the patient righteous will live to see what they were promised. "Keep his way" echoes Deut 8:6; 10:12; 11:22; 19:9 — the covenant path that leads to covenant blessing. "You will be there to see" (wĕrāʾîṯā) — the personal, embodied witness to divine justice; the righteous will not merely hear about the fall of the wicked but will see it themselves. This is the eschatological witness motif: those who have waited in faith will be vindicated by what they observe in the final settlement.
35: "I have seen a ruthless wicked man (ʾārîṣ) towering like a flourishing tree in its native ground (mitraʿănān kĕʾezrāḥ raʿănān)" — the tree metaphor for the wicked's flourishing reverses the blessed person's tree image in Ps 1:3 (the righteous as a tree planted by water). The wicked's tree is ʾezrāḥ raʿănān — a "green native tree," well-established in its natural soil, the picture of maximum vitality. The word ʾezrāḥ (native, indigenous, "one who arises from the soil") is the word for natural-born citizens vs. resident aliens — the wicked is rooted, established, the indigenous power. ʾĀrîṣ (ruthless, violent tyrant) is used in Isa 13:11; 49:25 for the aggressive oppressor.
36: "But he passed away — gone; I looked for him and he could not be found" — the counterpoint to v. 35: the tree that appeared immovable is simply gone. The brevity of the two cola mirrors the brevity of the wicked's end. Wayyaʿabōr (and he passed away) — the verb ʿābar (to pass over, to pass through, to go away) is used for the passing of storms, seasons, and life. The inability to find the place of the wicked echoes v. 10 ("you will search for his place, but it will not be found") and the erasure of the wicked's trace. The autobiographical form ("I looked for him") makes the theodicy claim a personal testimony rather than abstract doctrine.
37: "Take note of the blameless person (ṯāmîm) and look at the upright (yāšār); the future of such a person is peace (šālôm)" — the penultimate section's positive portrait, in contrast to the tree-and-gone portrait of the wicked. Tāmîm (blameless, complete, whole) — the same word used of Noah (Gen 6:9), Abraham (Gen 17:1), and Job (Job 1:1, 8); it denotes covenantal integrity, not moral perfection. "Future (ʾaḥărît)" — the future, the end-outcome, the final state; the wisdom tradition was preoccupied with the contrast between the ʾaḥărît of the righteous and the ʾaḥărît of the wicked (Prov 23:18; 24:14, 20; Jer 17:11). "Peace (šālôm)" as the telos of the righteous life: not merely absence of conflict but wholeness, completion, the fulfillment of the human covenant vocation.
38: "But rebels (pōšĕʿîm) will all be destroyed together; the future (ʾaḥărît) of the wicked will be cut off" — pešaʿ (rebellion, transgression) denotes specifically the willful, defiant violation of covenant relationship — not inadvertent failure but deliberate breach. The term is used in prophetic indictments of Israel's willful apostasy (Amos 1-2; Isa 1:2, 28; 53:5, 8, 12). "Together (yaḥdāw)" — the solidarity of the wicked in destruction mirrors their solidarity in scheming (v. 12); they conspired together and will be destroyed together. "Future cut off" (same ʾaḥărît as v. 37) — the stark antithesis: šālôm versus erasure.
39: "The salvation (tĕšûʿāh) of the righteous comes from the LORD; he is their stronghold (māʿûzzām) in times of trouble" — the psalm moves toward its conclusion with a compact summary of covenant theology. Tĕšûʿāh (salvation, deliverance, victory) is the verbal noun of yāšaʿ (to save, to deliver), the root of the names Joshua and Jesus. Māʿûz (stronghold, fortress, refuge) — the same military architecture metaphor used throughout the Psalter for YHWH as the fortified city protecting those who shelter within (Pss 27:1; 28:8; 31:2, 4; Nah 1:7). The combination of tĕšûʿāh and māʿûz captures both the active rescue and the passive protection dimensions of divine salvation.
40: "The LORD helps (yaʿzĕrēm) and delivers them (wĕyĕpallĕṭēm); he rescues them from the wicked and saves them, because they take shelter (ḥāsû) in him" — the final verse of the acrostic summarizes in four verbs the divine activity: help, deliver, rescue, save. The final condition — "because they take shelter in him (kî-ḥāsû bô)" — places the decisive weight not on merit but on trust: what qualifies the righteous for divine rescue is their refuge-taking (the ḥāsāh-shelter verb, also in Pss 2:12; 5:11; 17:7; 25:20; 31:19; 34:22). The acrostic ends with this trust-based refuge as the comprehensive answer to the entire psalm's question: how do the righteous survive when the wicked prosper? By sheltering in the God who will ultimately cut the wicked off.
